title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Guardians of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Mahi Kaitiaki: Ngā Kaitiaki o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (our local stream), connecting to Ngāti Kahungunu narratives of guardianship. They will learn about historical practices of sustainable resource management and how these principles can address modern challenges like water quality and biodiversity loss in our specific catchment area.
learningObjectives: Analyze and explain the concept of kaitiakitanga and its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu in relation to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Investigate and record the current health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku by conducting water quality tests and ecological surveys.
Collaboratively design and propose a realistic, culturally-grounded action plan to enhance the mauri (life force) of our awa.
Effectively communicate findings and proposals using appropriate digital tools and te reo Māori kupu (words).

activities.title: He Kōrero Tawhito: The Story of Our Awa
activities.description: Students engage with a local kaumātua or representative from Mana Whenua who will share pūrākau (traditional stories) and mātauranga Māori (Māori knowledge) associated with Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. This is followed by a guided walk to significant sites along the awa to connect the narratives to the physical landscape.

activities.title: Te Tirohanga Ō-Nāianei: Assessing the Present
activities.description: In small groups, students become citizen scientists. Using testing kits, they measure key indicators of the awa's health (pH, nitrate levels, clarity, macroinvertebrate count). They document their findings through digital photos, videos, and data logs, creating a current snapshot of the awa.

activities.title: Ngā Raru me ngā Rongoā: Problems and Solutions
activities.description: Groups analyze their collected data to identify the main issues affecting the awa (e.g., pollution, sedimentation, invasive species). They then research both mātauranga Māori solutions (e.g., traditional planting for bank stability) and modern scientific approaches to address these specific problems.

activities.title: Te Mahere Mahi: The Action Plan
activities.description: Drawing on their research, each group develops a detailed Kaitiaki Action Plan for a specific section of the awa. The plan must include clear actions (e.g., organising a community planting day with harakeke/flax), a rationale rooted in kaitiakitanga, required resources, and a timeline.

activities.title: Te Whakapuaki: Presenting to the Mana Whenua
activities.description: Groups formally present their Action Plans to a panel comprising a school leader, a teacher, and a representative from the local marae or Mana Whenua group. They must advocate for their plan, explaining how it upholds the principles of kaitiakitanga and benefits the community.

resources: A local Kaumātua or representative from Ngāti Kahungunu iwi authorities
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) resources and digital data sheets
The digital platform 'NZ Freshwater Fish' for species identification and data
Te Ara - The Encyclopedia of New Zealand, specifically entries on 'Kaitiakitanga' and 'Traditional Māori Conservation'

tasks: A collaborative group report documenting the awa's current health data and analysis.
An individual written reflection on the personal meaning of kaitiakitanga and their role as a kaitiaki.
A group-developed digital presentation (e.g., slideshow, short video) pitching their Kaitiaki Action Plan.

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges.
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies.
Social Studies L4: Understand how people’s management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability.
Vision: Confident, connected, actively involved, lifelong learners.
Principles: Treaty of Waitangi, Cultural Diversity, Community Engagement.
Key Competencies: Participating and Contributing, Thinking, Using Language, Symbols and Texts.
